text: The surgeon begins the procedure with the mobilization of the small bowel, using a harmonic scalpel for dissection. The proximal and distal small bowel segments are divided with an endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel, performing an en bloc resection. The small bowel mesentery is dissected using a harmonic scalpel, and the proximal and distal sections of the small bowel are divided with an endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein at the DJ flexure and performs medial to lateral mobilization in front of the pancreas. The IMV is clipped with hemologs and cut between them. Traction and counter traction are applied to create a space in front of the pancreas, facilitating entry into the lesser sac.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein at the DJ flexure and performs medial to lateral mobilization in front of the pancreas. The IMV is clipped with hemologs and cut between them. Traction and counter traction are applied to create a space in front of the pancreas, facilitating entry into the lesser sac. A swab is placed on the pancreas to aid in further mobilization of the splenic flexure.

A swab is placed on the pancreas to aid in the mobilization of the splenic flexure. The surgeon identifies the groove of the inferior mesenteric artery and begins dissection at the sacral promontory, incising the peritoneum. Medial to lateral mobilization is performed, separating the mesocolon from retroperitoneal structures and isolating the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery groove and begins dissection at the sacral promontory, incising the peritoneum. Medial to lateral mobilization is performed to separate the mesocolon from retroperitoneal structures, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery. The artery is dissected, clipped with hemolock, and divided. Using hook diathermy, the surgeon continues medial to lateral mobilization of the left colon, ensuring precise dissection with traction and counter traction. The colon is fully mobilized medially, and lateral attachments are divided with hook diathermy.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to dissect the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), clipping it between hemolocks and dividing it. The left colon undergoes further medial to lateral mobilization, with precise dissection using hook diathermy. The colon is completely mobilized medially, and lateral attachments are divided with hook diathermy.

The surgeon begins splenic flexure mobilization by dividing the gastrocolic omentum and releasing the transverse colon, entering the lesser sac from above. The dissection proceeds laterally to release the splenocolic ligament, with a swab visibly placed above the pancreas to aid in the complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon begins splenic flexural mobilization by dividing the gastrocolic omentum and releasing the transverse colon, entering the lesser sac from above. The dissection proceeds laterally to release the splenocolic ligament, with a swab visibly placed above the pancreas, achieving complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection involving the urinary bladder, as indicated by the CT scan. The bladder wall is fully divided and removed with the specimen, emphasizing the use of traction and counter traction during the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection involving the urinary bladder, as indicated by the CT scan. The full thickness of the bladder wall is carefully divided and removed with the specimen, utilizing traction and counter traction for precise laparoscopic manipulation.

The surgeon carefully separates the left iliac artery, enters the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, and performs an en bloc resection of the specimen, including the small bowel and urinary bladder wall.

The left iliac artery is carefully separated, and the surgeon enters the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The specimen, including the small bowel and urinary bladder wall, is resected en bloc. The sigmoid colon is prepared for transection using a harmonic scalpel and divided with an endo-GIA-60 stapler. The resected tumor specimen is extracted through the midline incision using an Alexis wound protector, followed by an examination of the specimen.
